title: Shoot 'em ups

infobox.value: (also known as **shmups** or, chiefly in Japan, **STGs**)[[1]](#cite_note-davies08-1)[[2]](#cite_note-carless11-2) are a [subgenre](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre") of [action games](/wiki/Action_games "Action games"). There is no consensus as to which design elements compose a shoot 'em up; some restrict the definition to games featuring spacecraft and certain types of character movement, while others allow a broader definition including characters on foot and a variety of perspectives.

restrict the player and enemies to a single screen, and the player primarily moves along a single axis, such as back and forth along the bottom of the screen.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Examples include *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* (1978), *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* (1979), *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(1980_video_game) "Phoenix (1980 video game)")* (1980), and *[Galaga](/wiki/Galaga "Galaga")* (1981). In *[Pooyan](/wiki/Pooyan "Pooyan")* (1982), the fixed axis of movement is vertical, along the right side of the screen. In *[Centipede](/wiki/Centipede_(video_game) "Centipede (video game)")* (1981) and *[Gorf](/wiki/Gorf "Gorf")* (1981), the player primarily moves left and right along the bottom, but several inches of vertical motion are also allowed within an invisible box.

allow 360-degree movement where the protagonist may rotate and move in any direction[[14]](#cite_note-14) such as *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")* (1979) and *[Mad Planets](/wiki/Mad_Planets "Mad Planets")* (1983). Multidirectional shooters with one joystick for movement and one joystick for firing in any direction independent of movement are called [twin-stick shooters](/wiki/Twin-stick_shooter "Twin-stick shooter"). One of the first games to popularize twin-stick controls was *[Robotron: 2084](/wiki/Robotron:_2084 "Robotron: 2084")* (1982).[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

are a thematic variant of involving [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"). Following the success of *Space Invaders*, space shooters were the dominant subgenre during the late 1970s to early 1980s.[[17]](#cite_note-RG80s-17) These games can overlap with other subgenres as well as [space combat games](/wiki/Space_combat_game "Space combat game").

**Shoot 'em ups** (also known as **shmups** or, chiefly in Japan, **STGs**)[[1]](#cite_note-davies08-1)[[2]](#cite_note-carless11-2) are a [subgenre](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre") of [action games](/wiki/Action_games "Action games"). There is no consensus as to which design elements compose a shoot 'em up; some restrict the definition to games featuring spacecraft and certain types of character movement, while others allow a broader definition including characters on foot and a variety of perspectives.

The genre's roots can be traced back to earlier [shooting games](/wiki/Shooting_games "Shooting games"), including target shooting [electro-mechanical games](/wiki/Electro-mechanical_games "Electro-mechanical games") of the mid-20th-century, but did not receive a video game release until *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962). The shoot 'em up genre was established by the hit [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, which popularised and set the general template for the genre in 1978, and has spawned many clones. The genre was then further developed by arcade hits such as *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")* and *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* in 1979. Shoot 'em ups were popular throughout the 1980s to early 1990s, diversifying into a variety of subgenres such as [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") shooters, run and gun games and rail shooters. In the mid-1990s, shoot 'em ups became a niche genre based on design conventions established in the 1980s, and increasingly catered to specialist enthusiasts, particularly in Japan. "[Bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell")" games are a subgenre of shooters that features overwhelming numbers of enemy [projectiles](/wiki/Projectiles "Projectiles"), often in visually impressive formations.

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

A "shoot 'em up", also known as a "shmup"[[3]](#cite_note-igntop10-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Jets'n'Guns-4) or "STG" (the common Japanese abbreviation for "shooting games"),[[1]](#cite_note-davies08-1)[[2]](#cite_note-carless11-2) is a game in which the protagonist combats a large number of enemies by shooting at them while dodging their fire. The controlling player must rely primarily on reaction times to succeed.[[5]](#cite_note-ashcraft70-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6) Beyond this, critics differ on exactly which design elements constitute a shoot 'em up. Some restrict the genre to games featuring some kind of craft, using fixed or [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") movement.[[5]](#cite_note-ashcraft70-5) Others widen the scope to include games featuring such protagonists as robots or humans on foot, as well as including games featuring "on-rails" (or "into the screen") and "run and gun" movement.[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6)[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[8]](#cite_note-bloodywolf-8) Mark Wolf restricts the definition to games featuring multiple antagonists ("'em" being short for "them"), calling games featuring one-on-one shooting "combat games".[[9]](#cite_note-9) Formerly, critics described any game where the primary design element was shooting as a "shoot 'em up",[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6) but later shoot 'em ups became a specific, inward-looking genre based on design conventions established in those shooting games of the 1980s.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)

### Common elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Common elements")]

Shoot 'em ups are a subgenre of [action games](/wiki/Action_game "Action game"). These games are usually viewed from a [top-down](/wiki/Video_game_graphics#Top-down_perspective "Video game graphics") or [side-view perspective](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game"), and players must use ranged weapons to take action at a distance. The player's [avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(computing) "Avatar (computing)") is typically a vehicle or spacecraft under constant attack. Thus, the player's goal is to shoot as quickly as possible at anything that moves or threatens them to reach the end of the level, usually with a [boss battle](/wiki/Boss_battle "Boss battle").[[10]](#cite_note-fundamentals-10) In some games, the player's character can withstand some damage or a single hit will result in their destruction.[[4]](#cite_note-Jets'n'Guns-4) The main skills required in shoot 'em ups are fast reactions and memorising enemy attack patterns. Some games feature overwhelming numbers of enemy projectiles and the player has to memorise their patterns to survive. These games belong to one of the fastest-paced [video game genres](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre").

Large numbers of enemy characters programmed to behave in an easily predictable manner are typically featured.[[11]](#cite_note-11) These enemies may behave in a certain way dependent on their type, or attack in formations that the player can learn to predict. The basic gameplay tends to be straightforward with many varieties of weapons.[[4]](#cite_note-Jets'n'Guns-4) Shoot 'em ups rarely have realistic physics. Characters can instantly change direction with no [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia"), and projectiles move in a straight line at constant speeds.[[10]](#cite_note-fundamentals-10) Lasers and other energy beams may make right-angle turns or may "track" the movement of the ship that fired them. The player's character can collect "[power-ups](/wiki/Power-up "Power-up")" which may afford the character's greater protection, an "[extra life](/wiki/Life_(video_games)#Extra_lives "Life (video games)")", health, shield, or upgraded weaponry.[[12]](#cite_note-gradiuscollection-12) Different weapons are often suited to different enemies, but these games seldom keep track of ammunition. As such, players tend to fire indiscriminately, and their weapons only damage legitimate targets.[[10]](#cite_note-fundamentals-10)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Types")]

Shoot 'em ups are categorized by their design elements, particularly viewpoint and movement:[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6)

**Fixed shooters** restrict the player and enemies to a single screen, and the player primarily moves along a single axis, such as back and forth along the bottom of the screen.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Examples include *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* (1978), *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* (1979), *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(1980_video_game) "Phoenix (1980 video game)")* (1980), and *[Galaga](/wiki/Galaga "Galaga")* (1981). In *[Pooyan](/wiki/Pooyan "Pooyan")* (1982), the fixed axis of movement is vertical, along the right side of the screen. In *[Centipede](/wiki/Centipede_(video_game) "Centipede (video game)")* (1981) and *[Gorf](/wiki/Gorf "Gorf")* (1981), the player primarily moves left and right along the bottom, but several inches of vertical motion are also allowed within an invisible box.

**Multidirectional shooters** allow 360-degree movement where the protagonist may rotate and move in any direction[[14]](#cite_note-14) such as *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")* (1979) and *[Mad Planets](/wiki/Mad_Planets "Mad Planets")* (1983). Multidirectional shooters with one joystick for movement and one joystick for firing in any direction independent of movement are called [twin-stick shooters](/wiki/Twin-stick_shooter "Twin-stick shooter"). One of the first games to popularize twin-stick controls was *[Robotron: 2084](/wiki/Robotron:_2084 "Robotron: 2084")* (1982).[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Project_Starfighter.png/250px-Project_Starfighter.png)](/wiki/File:Project_Starfighter.png)

*Project Starfighter*, a side-view space shooter

**Space shooters** are a thematic variant of involving [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"). Following the success of *Space Invaders*, space shooters were the dominant subgenre during the late 1970s to early 1980s.[[17]](#cite_note-RG80s-17) These games can overlap with other subgenres as well as [space combat games](/wiki/Space_combat_game "Space combat game").

**Tube shooters** feature craft flying through an abstract tube,[[18]](#cite_note-18) such as *[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(video_game) "Tempest (video game)")* (1981) and *[Gyruss](/wiki/Gyruss "Gyruss")* (1983). There is still a single axis of motion, making these a subset of fixed shooters.

**[Rail shooters](/wiki/Rail_shooter "Rail shooter")** limit the player to moving around the screen while following a specific route;[[19]](#cite_note-panzer-19) these games often feature an "into the screen" viewpoint, with which the action is seen from behind the [player character](/wiki/Player_character "Player character"), and moves "into the screen", while the player retains control over dodging.[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Examples include *[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")* (1985), *[Captain Skyhawk](/wiki/Captain_Skyhawk "Captain Skyhawk")* (1990), *[Starblade](/wiki/Starblade "Starblade")* (1991), *[Star Fox](/wiki/Star_Fox_(1993_video_game) "Star Fox (1993 video game)")* (1993), *[Star Wars: Rebel Assault](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Rebel_Assault "Star Wars: Rebel Assault")* (1993), *[Panzer Dragoon](/wiki/Panzer_Dragoon_(video_game) "Panzer Dragoon (video game)")* (1995), and *[Sin and Punishment](/wiki/Sin_and_Punishment "Sin and Punishment")* (2000). Rail shooters that use [light guns](/wiki/Light_guns "Light guns") are called *[light gun shooters](/wiki/Light_gun_shooter "Light gun shooter")*, such as *[Operation Wolf](/wiki/Operation_Wolf "Operation Wolf")* (1987), *[Lethal Enforcers](/wiki/Lethal_Enforcers "Lethal Enforcers")* (1992), *[Virtua Cop](/wiki/Virtua_Cop "Virtua Cop")* (1994), *[Point Blank](/wiki/Point_Blank_(video_game_series) "Point Blank (video game series)")* (1994), *[Time Crisis](/wiki/Time_Crisis_(video_game) "Time Crisis (video game)")* (1995), *[The House of the Dead](/wiki/The_House_of_the_Dead_(video_game) "The House of the Dead (video game)")* (1997) and *[Elemental Gearbolt](/wiki/Elemental_Gearbolt "Elemental Gearbolt")* (1997). Light-gun games that are "on rails" are usually not considered to be in the shoot-em-up category, but rather their own first-person light-gun shooter category.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

**Cute 'em ups** feature brightly colored graphics depicting surreal settings and enemies. Cute 'em ups tend to have unusual, oftentimes completely bizarre opponents for the player to fight, with *[Twinbee](/wiki/TwinBee_(series) "TwinBee (series)")* and *[Fantasy Zone](/wiki/Fantasy_Zone "Fantasy Zone")* first pioneering the subgenre,[[22]](#cite_note-22) along with *[Parodius](/wiki/Parodius "Parodius")*, *[Cotton](/wiki/Cotton_(series) "Cotton (series)")*, and *[Harmful Park](/wiki/Harmful_Park "Harmful Park")* being additional key games.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Some cute 'em ups may employ overtly sexual characters and innuendo.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Scrolling shooters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Scrolling shooters")]

**[Vertically scrolling shooters](/wiki/Vertically_scrolling_video_game#Vertically_scrolling_shooters "Vertically scrolling video game")** present the action from [above](/wiki/Video_game_graphics#Top-down_perspective "Video game graphics") and scroll up (or occasionally down) the screen.

**[Horizontally scrolling shooters](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game")** usually present a side-on view and scroll left to right (or less often, right to left).[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6)[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

**Isometrically scrolling shooters** or **isometric shooters**, such as [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s *[Zaxxon](/wiki/Zaxxon "Zaxxon")* (1981), use an [isometric point of view](/wiki/Isometric_video_game_graphics "Isometric video game graphics").[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)

A popular implementation style of scrolling shooters has the player's flying vehicle moving forward, at a fixed rate, through an environment. Examples are *[Scramble](/wiki/Scramble_(video_game) "Scramble (video game)")* (1981), *[Xevious](/wiki/Xevious "Xevious")* (1983), *[Gradius](/wiki/Gradius_(video_game) "Gradius (video game)")* (1985), *[Darius](/wiki/Darius_(video_game) "Darius (video game)")* (1987), *[R-Type](/wiki/R-Type "R-Type")* (1987), *[Einhänder](/wiki/Einh%C3%A4nder "Einhänder")* (1997). In contrast, *[Defender](/wiki/Defender_(video_game) "Defender (video game)")* (1981) allows the player to move left or right at will.

**Run and gun** games have protagonists that move through the world on foot and shoot attackers. Examples include the vertically scrolling, overhead view games *[Front Line](/wiki/Front_Line_(video_game) "Front Line (video game)")* (1982), *[Commando](/wiki/Commando_(video_game) "Commando (video game)")* (1985), and *[Ikari Warriors](/wiki/Ikari_Warriors "Ikari Warriors")* (1986). Side-scrolling run and gun games often combine elements from [platform games](/wiki/Platform_games "Platform games"), such as the ability to jump: *[Contra](/wiki/Contra_(video_game) "Contra (video game)")* (1987), *[Metal Slug](/wiki/Metal_Slug_(1996_video_game) "Metal Slug (1996 video game)")* (1996) and *[Cuphead](/wiki/Cuphead "Cuphead")* (2017). Run and gun games may also use [isometric viewpoints](/wiki/Isometric_projection "Isometric projection") and may have multidirectional movement.[[26]](#cite_note-bloodywolf2-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-ysguide2-28)

### Bullet hell

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bullet hell")]

Main article: [Bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/8/86/ARC_Mushihimesama.png)](/wiki/File:ARC_Mushihimesama.png)

*[Mushihimesama](/wiki/Mushihimesama "Mushihimesama")* (2004), a bullet hell game

**Bullet hell** (弾幕, *danmaku*; literally "barrage" or "bullet curtain") is a subgenre of shooters in which the screen becomes crowded with complex "curtain fire" enemy patterns. It is also characterized by collision boxes that are smaller than the sprites themselves, to accommodate maneuvering through these crowded firing patterns.[[29]](#cite_note-ashcraft66-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) This style of game, also known as "manic shooters"[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7) or "maniac shooters",[[31]](#cite_note-ashcraft77-31) originated in the mid-1990s as an offshoot of scrolling shooters.[[31]](#cite_note-ashcraft77-31) The *[DonPachi](/wiki/DonPachi "DonPachi")* and *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")* series are early titles establishing the principle of bullet hells.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

A **bullet heaven** or **reverse bullet hell** is a subgenre characterized by the player character collecting or unlocking abilities and attacks whose visuals overlap and clutter the game screen as the game progresses. They also share a feature of many enemy characters, commonly called "hordes", walking toward the player from off-screen. This genre is generally attributed to *[Vampire Survivors](/wiki/Vampire_Survivors "Vampire Survivors")*, released in 2022.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Trance shooters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trance shooters")]

A small subgenre of shooter games that emphasizes chaotic, reflex-based gameplay designed to put the player in a trance-like state. In trance shooters, enemy patterns usually have randomized elements, forcing the player to rely on reflexes rather than pattern memorization. Games of this type usually feature colorful, abstract visuals, and electronic music (often [techno music](/wiki/Techno_music "Techno music")). [Jeff Minter](/wiki/Jeff_Minter "Jeff Minter") is commonly credited with originating the style with *[Tempest 2000](/wiki/Tempest_2000 "Tempest 2000")* (1994) and subsequent games including *[Space Giraffe](/wiki/Space_Giraffe "Space Giraffe")*, *[Gridrunner++](/wiki/Gridrunner%2B%2B "Gridrunner++")*, and *[Polybius](/wiki/Polybius_(2017_video_game) "Polybius (2017 video game)")* (2017). Other examples include the *[Geometry Wars](/wiki/Geometry_Wars "Geometry Wars")* series, *[Space Invaders Extreme](/wiki/Space_Invaders_Extreme "Space Invaders Extreme")*, *[Super Stardust HD](/wiki/Super_Stardust_HD "Super Stardust HD")*, and *[Resogun](/wiki/Resogun "Resogun")*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Spacewar1.png/250px-Spacewar1.png)](/wiki/File:Spacewar1.png)

*[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962), an [early mainframe game](/wiki/Early_mainframe_game "Early mainframe game") with shooting and spacecraft

The concept of [shooting games](/wiki/Shooting_games "Shooting games") existed before [video games](/wiki/Video_games "Video games"), dating back to shooting gallery [carnival games](/wiki/Carnival_game "Carnival game") in the late 19th century[[34]](#cite_note-routledge_chp31-34) and [target sports](/wiki/Target_sports "Target sports") such as [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery"), [bowling](/wiki/Bowling "Bowling") and [darts](/wiki/Darts "Darts"). Mechanical target shooting games first appeared in [England](/wiki/England "England")'s [amusement arcades](/wiki/Amusement_arcades "Amusement arcades") around the turn of the 20th century,[[35]](#cite_note-35) before appearing in America by the 1920s.[[36]](#cite_note-ashcraft145-36) Shooting gallery games eventually evolved into more sophisticated target shooting [electro-mechanical games](/wiki/Electro-mechanical_game "Electro-mechanical game") (EM games) such as [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s influential *[Periscope](/wiki/Periscope_(arcade_game) "Periscope (arcade game)")* (1965). Shooting video games have roots in EM shooting games.[[34]](#cite_note-routledge_chp31-34)[[37]](#cite_note-Williams-37)

[Video game journalist](/wiki/Video_game_journalism "Video game journalism") Brian Ashcraft argues the [early mainframe game](/wiki/Early_mainframe_game "Early mainframe game") *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")* (1962) was the first shoot 'em up video game.[[38]](#cite_note-ashcraft72-38) It was developed at the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") in 1961, for the developers' amusement, and presents a space battle between two craft. It was remade four times as an arcade video game in the 1970s.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Emergence of shoot 'em up genre (late 1970s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Emergence of shoot 'em up genre (late 1970s)")]

*[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* (1978) is most frequently cited as the "first" or "original" in the genre.[[6]](#cite_note-ysguide-6)[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Space_Invaders-41) A seminal game created by [Tomohiro Nishikado](/wiki/Tomohiro_Nishikado "Tomohiro Nishikado") of Japan's [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito"), it led to proliferation of shooter games.[[42]](#cite_note-1UP-10things-42) It pitted the player against multiple enemies descending from the top of the screen at a constantly increasing speed.[[41]](#cite_note-Space_Invaders-41) Nishikado conceived the game by combining elements of *[Breakout](/wiki/Breakout_(video_game) "Breakout (video game)")* (1976) with those of earlier target shooting games, and simple alien creatures inspired by [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells")' *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")*. The hardware was unable to render the movement of aircraft, so the game was set in space, with a black background. It had a more interactive style of play than earlier target shooting games, with multiple enemies who responded to the player-controlled cannon's movement and fired back at the player. The game ended when the player was killed by the enemies.[[43]](#cite_note-RG-3-43)[[37]](#cite_note-Williams-37) While earlier shooting games allowed the player to shoot at targets, *Space Invaders* was the first where multiple enemies fired back at the player.[[44]](#cite_note-Nikkei-44) It also introduced the idea of giving the player multiple [lives](/wiki/Life_(video_games) "Life (video games)")[[45]](#cite_note-45) and popularized the concept of achieving a [high score](/wiki/High_score "High score").[[46]](#cite_note-IGN-10-46)[[47]](#cite_note-GS-SI-Hall-47)[[48]](#cite_note-GWR08-2-48)

With these elements, *Space Invaders* set the general template for the shoot 'em up genre.[[49]](#cite_note-1UP-50-49) It became one of the most widely cloned shooting games, spawning more than 100 imitators with only the most minor differences (if any) from the original.[[50]](#cite_note-smithsonianmag_history-50) Most shooting games released since then[[42]](#cite_note-1UP-10things-42) have followed its "multiple life, progressively difficult [level](/wiki/Level_(video_games) "Level (video games)")" paradigm, according to [Eugene Jarvis](/wiki/Eugene_Jarvis "Eugene Jarvis").[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Golden age and refinement (late 1970s to early 1980s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Golden age and refinement (late 1970s to early 1980s)")]

See also: [Golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games")

Following the success of *Space Invaders*, shoot 'em ups became the dominant genre for much of the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games"), from the late 1970s up until the early 1980s, particularly the "space shooter" subgenre.[[17]](#cite_note-RG80s-17) In 1979, [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco")'s *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")*—"the granddaddy of all top-down shooters", according to IGN—was released.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Its use of colour graphics and individualised antagonists were considered "strong evolutionary concepts" among space ship games.[[53]](#cite_note-joystick1_10-53) In 1981 *[Gorf](/wiki/Gorf "Gorf")* brought joystick control and (limited) vertical as well as horizontal movement to the vertically-oriented fixed-shooter genre, while *Space Invaders* and *Galaxian* have only horizontal movement controlled by a pair of buttons.[[54]](#cite_note-blanchet_gorf_joystick-54)[[55]](#cite_note-hirschfeld_gorf_joystick-55) [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari")'s *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")* (1979) was a hit multi-directional shooter, taking from *Spacewar!* the ability for the player's ship to roam the entire screen and to rotate, move and shoot in any direction.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The *Space Invaders* format evolved into the [vertical scrolling](/wiki/Vertical_scrolling "Vertical scrolling") shooter sub-genre.[[37]](#cite_note-Williams-37) [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK")'s debut shoot 'em up *[Ozma Wars](/wiki/Ozma_Wars "Ozma Wars")* (1979) featured vertical scrolling backgrounds and enemies,[[57]](#cite_note-GameSpot-57) and it was the first [action game](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") to feature a supply of energy, similar to [hit points](/wiki/Hit_points "Hit points").[[58]](#cite_note-58) Namco's *[Xevious](/wiki/Xevious "Xevious")*, released in 1982, was one of the first and most influential vertical scrolling shooters.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7) *Xevious* is also the first to convincingly portray dithered/shaded organic landscapes as opposed to blocks-in-space or wireframe obstacles.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

[Side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling "Side-scrolling") shoot 'em ups emerged in the early 1980s. *[Defender](/wiki/Defender_(video_game) "Defender (video game)")*, released by [Williams Electronics](/wiki/Williams_Electronics "Williams Electronics") in 1981, allowed side-scrolling in both directions in a wrap-around game world, unlike most later games in the genre.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7) The [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") helped remove design limitations associated with the screen,[[60]](#cite_note-Joystik1-2-60) and it also featured a [minimap](/wiki/Minimap "Minimap") radar.[[61]](#cite_note-GS-Hall-61) *[Scramble](/wiki/Scramble_(video_game) "Scramble (video game)")*, released by [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami") in early 1981, had continuous scrolling in a single direction and was the first side-scrolling shooter with multiple distinct [levels](/wiki/Level_(video_games) "Level (video games)").[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)

In the early 1980s, Japanese arcade developers began moving away from space shooters towards character [action games](/wiki/Action_games "Action games"), whereas American arcade developers continued to focus on space shooters during the early 1980s, up until the end of the arcade golden age. According to [Eugene Jarvis](/wiki/Eugene_Jarvis "Eugene Jarvis"), American developers were greatly influenced by Japanese space shooters but took the genre in a different direction from the "more deterministic, scripted, pattern-type" gameplay of Japanese games, towards a more "programmer-centric design culture, emphasizing algorithmic generation of backgrounds and enemy dispatch" and "an emphasis on random-event generation, particle-effect explosions and physics" as seen in arcade games such as his own *Defender* and *[Robotron: 2084](/wiki/Robotron:_2084 "Robotron: 2084")* (1982) as well as Atari's *Asteroids* (1979).[[17]](#cite_note-RG80s-17) *Robotron: 2084* was an influential game in the multi-directional shooter subgenre.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Some games experimented with [pseudo-3D](/wiki/Pseudo-3D "Pseudo-3D") perspectives at the time. [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo")'s attempt at the genre, *[Radar Scope](/wiki/Radar_Scope "Radar Scope")* (1980), borrowed heavily from *Space Invaders* and *Galaxian*, but added a three-dimensional third-person perspective; the game was a commercial failure, however.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Atari's *[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(arcade_game) "Tempest (arcade game)")* (1981) was one of the earliest tube shooters and a more successful attempt to incorporate a 3D perspective into shooter games;[[65]](#cite_note-65) *Tempest* went on to influence several later rail shooters.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Sega's *[Zaxxon](/wiki/Zaxxon "Zaxxon")* (1981) introduced [isometric video game graphics](/wiki/Isometric_video_game_graphics "Isometric video game graphics") to the genre.[[17]](#cite_note-RG80s-17)

The term "shmup" is believed to have been coined in 1985 by the British [Commodore 64](/wiki/Commodore_64 "Commodore 64") magazine *[Zzap!64](/wiki/Zzap!64 "Zzap!64")*. In the July 1985 issue, the term was used by the editor Chris Anderson and reviewer [Julian Rignall](/wiki/Julian_Rignall "Julian Rignall").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

1985 saw the release of Konami's *[Gradius](/wiki/Gradius_(video_game) "Gradius (video game)")*, which gave the player greater control over the choice of weaponry, thus introducing another element of strategy.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7) The game also introduced the need for the player to memorise levels in order to achieve any measure of success.[[69]](#cite_note-ashcraft76-69) *Gradius*, with its iconic protagonist, defined the side-scrolling shoot 'em up and [spawned a series](/wiki/Gradius "Gradius") spanning several sequels.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The following year saw the emergence of one of Sega's forefront series with its game *[Fantasy Zone](/wiki/Fantasy_Zone "Fantasy Zone")*. The game received acclaim for its surreal graphics and setting and the protagonist, Opa-Opa, was for a time considered Sega's [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot").[[71]](#cite_note-71) The game borrowed *Defender's* device of allowing the player to control the direction of flight and along with the earlier *[TwinBee](/wiki/TwinBee "TwinBee")* (1985), is an early archetype of the "cute 'em up" subgenre.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[72]](#cite_note-72) In 1986, Taito released *[KiKi KaiKai](/wiki/KiKi_KaiKai "KiKi KaiKai")*, an overhead multidirectional shooter notable for using a traditional fantasy setting in contrast to most shoot 'em up games with science fiction motifs.[[73]](#cite_note-73) *[R-Type](/wiki/R-Type "R-Type")*, an acclaimed side-scrolling shoot 'em up, was released in 1987 by [Irem](/wiki/Irem "Irem"), employing slower paced scrolling than usual, with difficult, claustrophobic levels calling for methodical strategies.[[3]](#cite_note-igntop10-3)[[74]](#cite_note-74) 1990's *[Raiden](/wiki/Raiden_(arcade_game) "Raiden (arcade game)")* was the beginning of another acclaimed and enduring series to emerge from this period.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Run and gun and rail shooters (1980s to early 1990s)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Run and gun and rail shooters (1980s to early 1990s)")]

Run and gun games became popular in the mid-1980s. These games feature characters on foot, rather than spacecraft, and often have military themes. The origins of this type of shooter go back to *[Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff_(video_game) "Sheriff (video game)")* by [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), released in 1979.[[77]](#cite_note-77) [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK")'s *Sasuke vs. Commander* (1980), which had relatively detailed background graphics for its time, pit a [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") against a horde of [ninjas](/wiki/Ninjas "Ninjas"),[[78]](#cite_note-78) along with [boss fights](/wiki/Boss_fights "Boss fights").[[79]](#cite_note-79) Taito's *[Front Line](/wiki/Front_Line_(video_game) "Front Line (video game)")* (1982) introduced the [vertical scrolling](/wiki/Vertical_scrolling "Vertical scrolling") format later popularized by [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Commando](/wiki/Commando_(video_game) "Commando (video game)")* (1985), which established the standard formula used by later run and gun games.[[28]](#cite_note-ysguide2-28)[[80]](#cite_note-HG101-80) Sega's *[Ninja Princess](/wiki/Ninja_Princess "Ninja Princess")* (1985), which released slightly before *Commando*, was a run and gun game that was distinctive for its [feudal Japan](/wiki/Feudal_Japan "Feudal Japan") setting and [female ninja](/wiki/Female_ninja "Female ninja") protagonist who throws [shuriken](/wiki/Shuriken "Shuriken") and knives.[[80]](#cite_note-HG101-80) [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK")'s *[TNK III](/wiki/TNK_III "TNK III")*, released later in 1985, combined the *Front Line* tank shooter format with unique rotary [joystick](/wiki/Joystick "Joystick") controls, which they later combined with *Commando*-inspired run and gun gameplay to develop *[Ikari Warriors](/wiki/Ikari_Warriors "Ikari Warriors")* (1986), which further popularized run and gun shooters.[[81]](#cite_note-Continue-81) *Ikari Warriors* also drew inspiration from the [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") *[Rambo: First Blood Part II](/wiki/Rambo:_First_Blood_Part_II "Rambo: First Blood Part II")* (1985),[[57]](#cite_note-GameSpot-57) which it was originally intended to be an adaptation of.[[81]](#cite_note-Continue-81) Contemporary critics considered military themes and protagonists similar to [Rambo](/wiki/John_Rambo "John Rambo") or [Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") prerequisites for a shoot 'em up, as opposed to an [action-adventure game](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game").[[28]](#cite_note-ysguide2-28)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The success of *Commando* and *Ikari Warriors* led to run and gun games becoming the dominant style of shoot 'em up during the late 1980s to early 1990s, with the term "shoot 'em up" itself becoming synonymous with "run and gun" during this period.[[28]](#cite_note-ysguide2-28)

[Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s *[Green Beret](/wiki/Rush%27n_Attack "Rush'n Attack")* (1985), known as *Rush'n Attack* in North America, adapted the *Commando* formula to a [side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling "Side-scrolling") format.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Later notable side-scrolling run and gun shooters include Namco's *[Rolling Thunder](/wiki/Rolling_Thunder_(video_game) "Rolling Thunder (video game)")* (1986), which added [cover mechanics](/wiki/Cover_system "Cover system") to the formula,[[84]](#cite_note-denofgeek-84) and Data East's *[RoboCop](/wiki/RoboCop_(1988_video_game) "RoboCop (1988 video game)")* (1988).[[28]](#cite_note-ysguide2-28) In 1987, Konami created *[Contra](/wiki/Contra_(video_game) "Contra (video game)")*, a side-scrolling coin-op arcade game, and later a [NES](/wiki/NES "NES") game, that was particularly acclaimed for its multi-directional aiming and two-player cooperative gameplay. By the early 1990s and the [popularity of 16-bit consoles](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)"), the scrolling shooter genre was overcrowded, with developers struggling to make their games stand out, with exceptions such as the inventive *[Gunstar Heroes](/wiki/Gunstar_Heroes "Gunstar Heroes")* (1993) by [Treasure](/wiki/Treasure_(company) "Treasure (company)").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Sega's [pseudo-3D](/wiki/Pseudo-3D "Pseudo-3D") [rail shooter](/wiki/Rail_shooter "Rail shooter") *[Buck Rogers: Planet of Zoom](/wiki/Buck_Rogers:_Planet_of_Zoom "Buck Rogers: Planet of Zoom")* demonstrated the potential of [3D](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") shoot 'em up gameplay in 1982.[[86]](#cite_note-VideoGames-86) Sega's *[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")*, a rail shooter released in 1985, broke new ground graphically and its wide variety of settings across multiple levels gave players more to aim for than high scores.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-1upharrier-88) In 1986, [Arsys Software](/wiki/Arsys_Software "Arsys Software") released *[WiBArm](/wiki/Wibarm "Wibarm")*, a shooter that switched between a [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") side-scrolling view in outdoor areas to a fully 3D polygonal [third-person](/wiki/Third-person_(video_games) "Third-person (video games)") perspective inside buildings, while bosses were fought in an arena-style 2D battle, with the game featuring a variety of weapons and equipment.[[89]](#cite_note-Retro-89) In 1987, Square's *[3-D WorldRunner](/wiki/3-D_WorldRunner "3-D WorldRunner")* was an early stereoscopic 3-D shooter played from a third-person perspective,[[90]](#cite_note-90) followed later that year by its sequel *[JJ](/wiki/JJ_(video_game) "JJ (video game)")*,[[91]](#cite_note-91) and the following year by *[Space Harrier 3-D](/wiki/Space_Harrier_3-D "Space Harrier 3-D")* which used the SegaScope [3-D shutter glasses](/wiki/Liquid_crystal_shutter_glasses "Liquid crystal shutter glasses").[[92]](#cite_note-92) That same year, Sega's *[Thunder Blade](/wiki/Thunder_Blade "Thunder Blade")* switched between both a top-down view and a third-person view, and featured the use of [force feedback](/wiki/Haptic_technology "Haptic technology"), where the joystick vibrates.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Bullet hell and niche appeal (mid-1990s to present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shoot_%27em_up&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bullet hell and niche appeal (mid-1990s to present)")]

Main article: [Bullet hell](/wiki/Bullet_hell "Bullet hell")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(August 2017)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg/250px-Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg)

Japanese players at a shoot 'em up arcade in [Akihabara, Tokyo](/wiki/Akihabara "Akihabara") (2017)

Over the course of the 1990s, a new subgenre of shooters evolved, known as "*danmaku* (弾幕; "barrage") in Japan, and often referred to as "bullet hell" or "manic shooters" in English-speaking regions. These games are characterized by high numbers of enemy projectiles, often in complex "curtain fire" patterns, as well as collision boxes that are smaller than the sprites themselves, allowing the player to fit between the narrow gaps in enemy fire.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[31]](#cite_note-ashcraft77-31)

Bullet hell games were first popularized in Japanese arcades during a time when 3D games and fighting games were eclipsing other games. The flashy firing patterns were intended to grab players attention.[[31]](#cite_note-ashcraft77-31) [Toaplan](/wiki/Toaplan "Toaplan")'s *[Batsugun](/wiki/Batsugun "Batsugun")* (1993) is often considered a pivotal point in the development of this subgenre.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) After the closure of Toaplan, the following year, a number of studios formed from former Toaplan staff that would continue to develop this style, including [Cave](/wiki/Cave_(company) "Cave (company)") (formed by *Batsugun's* main creator Tsuneki Ikeda) who released 1995's seminal *[DonPachi](/wiki/DonPachi "DonPachi")*, and Takumi, who would develop the *GigaWing* series.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Bullet hell games marked another point where the shooter genre began to cater to more dedicated players.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[31]](#cite_note-ashcraft77-31) Games such as *Gradius* had been more difficult than *Space Invaders* or *Xevious*,[[69]](#cite_note-ashcraft76-69) but bullet hell games were yet more inward-looking and aimed at dedicated fans of the genre looking for greater challenges.[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[105]](#cite_note-105) While shooter games featuring protagonists on foot largely moved to 3D-based genres, popular, long-running series such as *[Contra](/wiki/Contra_(series) "Contra (series)")* and *[Metal Slug](/wiki/Metal_Slug_(series) "Metal Slug (series)")* continued to receive new sequels.[[106]](#cite_note-contraDS-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) Rail shooters have rarely been released in the new millennium, with only *[Rez](/wiki/Rez_(video_game) "Rez (video game)")* and *[Panzer Dragoon Orta](/wiki/Panzer_Dragoon_Orta "Panzer Dragoon Orta")* achieving cult recognition.[[19]](#cite_note-panzer-19)[[88]](#cite_note-1upharrier-88)[[109]](#cite_note-109) In the early 2000s, the genre achieved recognition through the mobile game *[Space Impact](/wiki/Space_Impact "Space Impact")*, which is considered one of the important games in the [history of mobile games](/wiki/History_of_mobile_games "History of mobile games").[[110]](#cite_note-cnet.com-110)

Treasure's shoot 'em up, *[Radiant Silvergun](/wiki/Radiant_Silvergun "Radiant Silvergun")* (1998), introduced an element of narrative to the genre. It was critically acclaimed for its refined design, though it was not released outside Japan and remains a much sought-after collector's item.[[3]](#cite_note-igntop10-3)[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) Its successor *[Ikaruga](/wiki/Ikaruga "Ikaruga")* (2001) featured improved graphics and was again acclaimed as one of the best games in the genre. Both *Radiant Silvergun* and *Ikaruga* were later released on [Xbox Live Arcade](/wiki/Xbox_Live_Arcade "Xbox Live Arcade").[[3]](#cite_note-igntop10-3)[[7]](#cite_note-lecture-7)[[113]](#cite_note-2dspace-113) The *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")* series spans 28 years and 32 games as of 2024 and was listed in the [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") in October 2010 for being the "most prolific fan-made shooter series".[[114]](#cite_note-114) The genre has undergone something of a resurgence with the release of the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") online services,[[113]](#cite_note-2dspace-113) while in Japan arcade shoot 'em ups retain a deep-rooted niche popularity.[[115]](#cite_note-ashcraft88-115) *[Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved](/wiki/Geometry_Wars:_Retro_Evolved "Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved")* was released on Xbox Live Arcade in 2005 and in particular stood out from the various re-releases and [casual games](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game") available on the service.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The PC has also seen its share of [dōjin](/wiki/D%C5%8Djin_soft "Dōjin soft") shoot 'em ups like *[Crimzon Clover](/wiki/Crimzon_Clover "Crimzon Clover")*, *[Jamestown: Legend of the Lost Colony](/wiki/Jamestown:_Legend_of_the_Lost_Colony "Jamestown: Legend of the Lost Colony")*, *Xenoslaive Overdrive*, and the [*eXceed* series](/wiki/EXceed_(series) "EXceed (series)"). However, despite the genre's continued appeal to an enthusiastic niche of players, shoot 'em up developers are increasingly embattled financially by the power of home consoles and their attendant genres.[[115]](#cite_note-ashcraft88-115)[[117]](#cite_note-117)
